title: Portfolio Website — Setup Guide
text:
Complete step-by-step from zero to live website.






You already have these. Skip if confirmed.



This is separate from the R package — it’s a command-line tool.
Go to: https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/ Download the installer for your OS (Linux/Windows/Mac) and run it.
Verify in your terminal / RStudio Terminal tab:
quarto --version
# should print something like: 1.4.553



install.packages("quarto")






Go to github.com and sign in as CarolGachema
Click New repository
Name it exactly: carolinegachema.github.io (this special name tells GitHub to host it as a website)
Set to Public
Tick Add a README file
Click Create repository

Then clone it to your computer:
git clone https://github.com/CarolGachema/carolinegachema.github.io.git
cd carolinegachema.github.io




Your folder should look like this after copying:
carolinegachema.github.io/
├── _quarto.yml
├── styles.scss
├── index.qmd
├── research.qmd
├── projects.qmd
├── policy.qmd
├── cv.qmd
├── images/
│   └── profile.jpg     ← add your photo here, named exactly this
└── SETUP.md            ← you can delete this once you're set up
Add your profile photo: Put a square-ish photo of yourself in the images/ folder, named profile.jpg.





In RStudio: File → Open Project → navigate to your folder → open carolinegachema.github.io.Rproj (or just open any .qmd file from that folder)
In the RStudio Terminal tab, run:

quarto preview

Your browser will open automatically at http://localhost:4321 You can edit files and the preview updates live.





Open each .qmd file and fill in your details where you see: - [YOUR PHOTO] → replaced by adding profile.jpg to images/ - [FILL IN: ...] → replace with your actual content - LinkedIn URL → update to your actual LinkedIn profile URL
Key things to update in _quarto.yml:
site-url: "https://carolinegachema.github.io"   # ← correct already
Key things to update in cv.qmd: - Your full education history - All experience entries




When you’re happy with the preview, build it:
quarto render
This creates a docs/ folder with your complete website.




git add .
git commit -m "Launch portfolio website"
git push origin main





Go to your repo on GitHub: github.com/CarolGachema/carolinegachema.github.io
Click Settings (top menu)
Click Pages (left sidebar)
Under Source, select Deploy from a branch
Branch: main · Folder: /docs
Click Save

Wait 2–3 minutes, then go to: https://carolinegachema.github.io
Your website is live. ✓




quarto render
git add .
git commit -m "Update: [what you changed]"
git push origin main
GitHub Pages updates automatically within 1–2 minutes.




“quarto: command not found” → Restart RStudio after installing Quarto CLI, then try again in the Terminal tab.
Website shows old content → Hard refresh: Ctrl+Shift+R (Windows) or Cmd+Shift+R (Mac)
Images not showing → Check file names are exact (case-sensitive). Profile.jpg ≠ profile.jpg
Fonts not loading → You need an internet connection when rendering. The fonts load from Google Fonts.

Questions? caroline@gachemacarol.gmail.com

title: Projects
section: Psychedelic Neuroplasticity and the Neurotrophic Architecture of Pancreatic Cancer (Convergent Pathways)
text:
Psychedelic Neuroplasticity and the Neurotrophic Architecture of Pancreatic Cancer (Convergent Pathways)




Translational Oncology
Bioinformatics


2026 · TCGA-PAAD


Overview
Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is highly aggressive and increasingly recognized to interact with the nervous system. Neurotrophin receptors such as NTRK1 and NTRK2 may influence tumour progression, yet their expression patterns in PDAC remain incompletely characterised.
TCGA-PAAD transcriptomic and survival data were analysed to investigate neurotrophin receptor expression, co-expression patterns, and clinical relevance.
Key Findings

Confirmed expression of both NTRK1 and NTRK2 across PDAC tumours
Demonstrated significant co-expression (Spearman ρ = 0.416, p < 0.001)
Showed MAPK and PI3K-Akt signalling pathways are significantly enriched in the NTRK2 co-expression network
Provided survival analysis by expression group


  
    
    Fig 1. NTRK1 and NTRK2 expression across 182 PDAC tumour samples
  
  
    
    Fig 2. Significant co-expression (ρ = 0.416, p < 0.001)
  


Key tools: R · clusterProfiler · survival · survminer · ggplot2 · UCSC Xena · TCGA

View GitHub Repository →    READ MORE →

title: Projects
text:
Alzheimer’s Disease Machine Learning Classifier




Machine Learning Neurodegeneration


2026 · ADNI Dataset


Overview
Alzheimer’s disease is the most common neurodegenerative disorder worldwide, yet its molecular mechanisms remain incompletely understood. This project aimed to identify key genes associated with Alzheimer’s disease and investigate whether gene expression signatures could be used to accurately classify disease status.
To evaluate the predictive potential of these genes, I developed multiple machine learning models in Python, including Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, Logistic Regression, and Gradient Boosting classifiers. Random Forest emerged as the best-performing model, achieving a cross-validation accuracy of 90.7%, test accuracy of 87.9%, and ROC-AUC of 0.9038.

  
    
    Fig 1. Top 50 Hub Genes in Alzheimers Disease
  
  
    
    Fig 2. ML Model Comparison
  

View GitHub Repository →

title: Projects
text:
Differential Gene Expression Pipelines




Genomics DEG Analysis


2025-2026


DEG analysis pipelines for two disease contexts: lung cancer and asthma.
Both pipelines follow a reproducible workflow from raw count data through to biological interpretation:
Lung Cancer Pipeline - Identified a set of significantly dysregulated genes associated with lung tumour biology. - Generated reproducible analytical pipelines for transcriptomic biomarker discovery. - Demonstrated the utility of publicly available genomic datasets for cancer research. - Significance The identification of differentially expressed genes may contribute to future biomarker discovery efforts and improve understanding of molecular pathways involved in lung cancer development and progression.
Asthma Pipeline - Identified genes significantly altered following corticosteroid treatment. - Generated functional enrichment profiles highlighting biological processes affected by therapy. - Developed a fully reproducible transcriptomic workflow for asthma treatment response analysis. - Significance Understanding molecular signatures associated with treatment response may contribute to personalised app

  
    
    Fig 1. Flovent vs Placebo analysis
  


Key tools: R · DESeq2 · clusterProfiler · ggplot2 · pheatmap · GEO

View GitHub Repository →

title: Projects
text:
Intersystem Noise Coupling




Signal Analysis Instrumentation


2026


Overview
This project proposes a novel computational framework called Intersystem Noise Coupling, which investigates whether biological “noise” the small fluctuations and variability present within physiological signals, may act as a mechanism of coordination between organ systems.
Rather than treating physiological noise as unwanted randomness, the framework explores the possibility that stochastic fluctuations carry meaningful information capable of synchronizing activity across multiple organs.
The project aims to utilise large-scale physiological datasets, including MMASH and MIMIC-IV, to analyse time-series signals from multiple organ systems simultaneously.
Approaches

Stochastic modelling
Cross-correlation analysis
Time-series decomposition
Network dynamics
Physiological synchronization metrics
Noise propagation modelling

MMASH Dataset
Heart rate and locomotion signals were modelled as coupled stochastic oscillators using an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (OU) process framework.


Figure 1. Time series and phase space portraits comparing an idealized healthy coupling scenario (κ_xy = 0.5, top row) against the MMASH dataset typical result (κ_xy = −0.0028, bottom row). The idealized system produces a structured synchronized attractor; the real data produces an independent random walk in phase space.

The idealized healthy simulation (κ_xy = 0.5) produces correlated heart-locomotion time series and a structured phase space attractor — the signature of two systems influencing each other.
The MMASH dataset, by contrast, yields a coupling coefficient of κ_xy = −0.0028: effectively zero. The phase space trajectory is an unstructured random walk, indicating that cardiac and locomotion signals in healthy wearable recordings behave as statistically independent processes at the timescales captured.


Figure 2. Windowed κ_xy (locomotion → cardiac, red) and κ_yx (cardiac → locomotion, blue dashed) across the recording period for four MMASH subjects. Coupling values are near zero throughout most of the day, with transient episodic spikes of variable direction.

The windowed analysis across subjects confirms this picture. For the majority of each recording, κ values for both directions hover near zero. Coupling is not absent by definition, brief transient episodes of stronger coupling appear episodically, and the dominant direction (locomotion-to-cardiac vs cardiac-to-locomotion) varies across subjects and time windows. Some subjects show sustained negative κ_xy during specific periods, suggesting inhibitory dynamics rather than simple uncoupling.

Key tools: Python · scikit-learn · pandas · numpy · matplotlib · ADNI

View GitHub Repository →

title: Caroline Gachema
text:
About Me

  
    
  
  
My work sits at the intersection of molecular biology, bioinformatics and global health policy. Combining hands-on laboratory experience with computational analysis to generate rigorous, reproducible results.
I am curious about the questions that sit on the edge of what we know, and how data can be made to serve the people it describes.

title: Policy Research
text: For equitable health policy for Africa

title: Policy Research
section: CCR5Δ32 Mutation Gap: The HIV Stem Cell Cure Is Nearly Irrelevant for Sub-Saharan Africa
text:
CCR5Δ32 Mutation Gap: The HIV Stem Cell Cure Is Nearly Irrelevant for Sub-Saharan Africa

Publishing in progress WHO-Directed 2025–2026


Overview
The paper is a structural and bio-ethical critique of the current global HIV cure paradigm. It argues that while the scientific community celebrates allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (allo-HSCT) as a landmark proof of concept, this “cure” is a biological and logistical illusion for Sub-Saharan Africa (SSA), the region that bears over two-thirds of the global HIV burden. The core thesis is that the cure is built on an accidental, Euro centric genetic framework that completely excludes, underfunds, and ignores the populations that need a HIV cure the most


The Problem

Complete Absence of the Allele - The cure relies entirely on a donor possessing the homozygous \(CCR5\Delta32\) genetic mutation, which stops HIV from entering host cells.


While this mutation exists in about 1% of Northern Europeans, large-scale genomic data shows its frequency is precisely 0% in studied populations across Angola, Nigeria, Zimbabwe, and Ethiopia.The HLA Matching Compound Problem:
Because successful transplants require strict Human Leukocyte Antigen (HLA) tissue matching to prevent fatal graft-versus-host disease, an African patient cannot simply use a European donor.


The Cost Wall - A single allo-HSCT procedure costs between $100,000 and $300,000. The procedure demands specialized sterile bone marrow units, advanced blood banking, high-resolution HLA typing tech, and intensive care units equipped for extreme toxicities. These resources are fundamentally absent or severely constrained in most Sub-Saharan healthcare systems.
The Cancer Requirement - This procedure has never been a standalone treatment for HIV; it is an incredibly aggressive, last-resort intervention for patients who also have life-threatening blood cancers (like leukemia).



Key Recommendations
The paper makes the following recommendations directed at the WHO and trial sponsors:

Commission an SSA - Focused Cure Agenda: Shift funding to study CCR5-independent remission mechanisms and invest in gene therapies with low-income access guidelines built into the initial trial phases.
Diversify Global Donor Registries - Launch targeted, well-funded recruitment drives for African, Asian, and Latin American donors to fix the massive European bias in global HLA matching data.
Establish Regional Centers of Excellence - Fund the construction of at least five regional advanced hematological and gene therapy medical hubs across Sub-Saharan Africa to serve both cure research and broader oncological needs.
Enforce Advance Market Commitments (AMCs) - Mandate tiered licensing agreements guaranteeing that when a viable, population-agnostic cure is found, it is affordable and accessible to LMICs immediately upon regulatory approval, preventing the multi-decade delay that occurred with early antiretroviral drugs.



Why This Matters
Because all human beings deserve treatment that works for them.

📄Full Paper

title: Policy Research
section: Neuropathological Consequences of Emerging Viral Pathogens in East Africa
text:
Neuropathological Consequences of Emerging Viral Pathogens in East Africa

Institute for Global Excellence Conference 2026


Overview
This review examined current evidence regarding the neuropathological effects of emerging viral diseases affecting East African populations, with particular attention to mechanisms of central nervous system injury, neuroinflammation, and long-term neurological sequelae.
The review synthesised findings from studies involving viral pathogens associated with neurological complications, including emerging infectious diseases capable of crossing the blood-brain barrier and inducing direct or indirect damage to neural tissues.
Key Themes - Neuroinflammation as a common mechanism of neurological injury. - Blood-brain barrier disruption during viral infection. - Direct viral invasion of neural tissue. - Long-term neurological and cognitive sequelae following infection. - Challenges in surveillance, diagnosis, and management within resource-limited settings.
Significance

The review highlights the need to expand infectious disease research beyond acute clinical outcomes and consider the long-term neurological burden associated with emerging pathogens.
By focusing on East Africa, the paper emphasizes regional research gaps and underscores the importance of strengthening neurological surveillance, research capacity, and healthcare preparedness for future outbreaks.




Interested in collaborating on health policy research?

Particularly interested in connecting with researchers and institutions working on African health equity, clinical trial design, and access to emerging therapies

Get in touch →

title: Research
section: Psychedelic Neuroplasticity and the Neurotrophic Architecture of Pancreatic Cancer (Convergent Pathways)
text:
Psychedelic Neuroplasticity and the Neurotrophic Architecture of Pancreatic Cancer (Convergent Pathways)

In Progress · 2026


Overview
Psilocybin-assisted therapy is entering oncology settings at scale: clinical trials now enrol patients with cancer-related psychological distress, and advancing Phase 3 data for treatment-resistant depression have accelerated its broader integration into cancer care. Its therapeutic mechanism has been substantially reframed by Moliner et al. (2023), who demonstrated that psilocin, the active metabolite of psilocybin, directly binds the TrkB receptor as a positive allosteric modulator, positioning neurotrophin signalling as the primary driver of psychedelic-induced neuroplasticity.
Concurrently, pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), a malignancy with near-universal perineural invasion (PNI) and a 12-month median survival post-diagnosis, is driven at the neural interface by nerve growth factor (NGF) signalling through TrkA, TrkB’s closest structural paralogue, sharing approximately 60% intracellular kinase domain identity and activating identical downstream effectors: MAPK/ERK, PI3K/Akt, and PLCγ. Critically, TrkB is also overexpressed in PDAC tumour cells, where it promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition and anoikis resistance.
This paper maps the structural and signalling convergence of TrkA and TrkB; reviews the emergence of cancer neuroscience as a field; traces psilocybin’s clinical trajectory in oncology populations; and proposes organoid-based neuro-tumour chip models and generative AI-driven pathway simulation as the minimum preclinical infrastructure required to characterise this interface before it is encountered in clinical practice.



Computational Validation: TCGA-PAAD Analysis
To provide empirical grounding for the molecular hypothesis, I analysed NTRK1 (TrkA) and NTRK2 (TrkB) expression across the TCGA-PAAD cohort (n = 182 pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma tumour samples).

Expression of NTRK1 and NTRK2 in PDAC
Both neurotrophin receptors are expressed across the full PDAC cohort, with NTRK1 showing a higher median expression (0.95 log₂ units) relative to NTRK2 (0.32 log₂ units). The right-skewed distribution of NTRK1 indicates a subset of tumours with very high TrkA expression, consistent with its established role in driving perineural invasion.


Figure 1. NTRK1 and NTRK2 expression across 182 PDAC tumour samples (TCGA-PAAD, HiSeqV2). Violin plots with embedded boxplot and individual data points. Median values annotated.



Co-expression of NTRK1 and NTRK2
A significant positive correlation exists between NTRK1 and NTRK2 expression across PDAC tumours (Spearman ρ = 0.416, p < 0.001). This co-expression pattern establishes that both receptors are simultaneously active within the same tumour microenvironmental context — a prerequisite for the cross-talk the paper proposes.


Figure 2. NTRK1–NTRK2 co-expression in TCGA-PAAD. Each point represents one tumour sample. Spearman ρ = 0.416, p < 0.001. Labelled samples: top 6 co-expressing tumours.



Survival Analysis
Kaplan-Meier analysis did not demonstrate a statistically significant association between NTRK2 expression and overall survival at median-split dichotomisation (HR = 0.89, 95% CI 0.59–1.33, log-rank p = 0.57), nor for NTRK1 (HR = 0.75, 95% CI 0.50–1.13, p = 0.17).
This null finding is consistent with the uniformly poor prognosis of PDAC across molecular subtypes and the limited statistical power of n = 182 for detecting moderate gene-level effects. The paper’s central argument is mechanistic rather than prognostic.


Pathway Enrichment
GSEA-KEGG analysis, with genes ranked by Spearman correlation with NTRK2 expression across all TCGA-PAAD samples, identified MAPK signalling and PI3K-Akt signalling as significantly positively enriched pathways in the NTRK2 co-expression network — precisely the downstream effectors shared between TrkA (perineural invasion) and TrkB (psilocin target).

  
    
      
      MAPK Signalling Pathway (hsa04010) — positively enriched in NTRK2 co-expression network
    
  
  
    
      
      PI3K-Akt Signalling Pathway (hsa04151) — positively enriched in NTRK2 co-expression network
    
  

📂 View full analysis on GitHub   ·   Data: UCSC Xena Browser

title: Research
section: Neuropathological Consequences of Emerging Viral Pathogens in East Africa:
text:
Neuropathological Consequences of Emerging Viral Pathogens in East Africa:

IGAE Conference · 2026


Overview
Emerging and re-emerging viral diseases pose a growing public health challenge in East Africa, yet much of the scientific focus has centered on acute infection and mortality rather than their long-term effects on the nervous system. This review explores how viral pathogens can affect the central nervous system through mechanisms such as neuroinflammation, blood-brain barrier disruption, immune-mediated injury, and direct viral invasion, resulting in neurological complications that may persist long after the initial infection has resolved.
Drawing on current evidence from the literature, the paper synthesizes the neuropathological mechanisms associated with emerging viral infections and highlights the implications for healthcare systems in East Africa. It identifies critical knowledge gaps in neurological surveillance, diagnosis, and long-term patient management while emphasizing the need for greater investment in neuroscience research within the region. The work reflects my broader interest in understanding disease mechanisms at both the molecular and systems levels, an interest that later informed my computational genomics projects.
###The Problem - Limited understanding of the long-term neurological consequences of emerging viral infections in East African populations. - Insufficient neurological surveillance and research infrastructure to monitor post-infectious neurological complications. - A lack of region-specific evidence to guide clinical management, public health policy, and preparedness for future outbreaks.


Conference Presentation
Abstract presented at the Insitute for Global Academic Excellence Conference, 2026.


  Interested in collaborating?
  Open to research partnerships, joint publications, and conversations about African health equity and computational biology
  ✉ gachemacarol@gmail.com
